retrieved_docs.title: Therapeutic Uses of Diuretic Agents -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Conivaptan and tolvaptan are not classified under diuretics but are called aquaretics due to their effects in increasing only the water excretion without any saluretic action of a diuretic. Conivaptan (IV route only) acts nonselectively on both V1A & V2 vasopressin receptors, whereas tolvaptan (oral agent) is a selective antagonist of V2 vasopressin receptors. Arginine vasopressin agonist action on its receptor leads to increased expression of luminal aquaporin-2 channels (thus promoting water reabsorption), whereas its antagonism by aquaretics leads to enhanced aquaresis. Aquaretics, with their electrolyte-sparing effect, are currently indicated for use only in hyponatremia. [89] [90]

retrieved_docs.title: Inotropes and Vasopressors -- Mechanism of Action -- Vasopressin
retrieved_docs.content: Vasopressin acts on V1 receptors to stimulate smooth muscle contraction of the vessels and as an anti-diuretic on V2 receptors in the kidneys. There are no inotropic or chronotropic effects. Only BP and SVR are increased with vasopressin administration. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Desmopressin -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Desmopressin is a selective vasopressin V2 receptor agonist present throughout the collecting ducts and distal convoluted tubules of the kidneys. [1] [18] The V2 receptor is a Gs-protein coupled receptor, which, when activated, results in a signaling cascade of adenyl-cyclase, prompting an increase in cyclic adenosine monophosphate (cAMP) in the renal tubule cells, ultimately resulting in increased water permeability. This activity leads to a decrease in urine volume and an increase in urine osmolality.

retrieved_docs.title: Syndrome of Inappropriate Antidiuretic Hormone Secretion -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Vasopressin receptor antagonists such as conivaptan (IV) or tolvaptan (oral) are also available and approved for severe persistent SIADH. These drugs prevent ADH-mediated free water retention by antagonizing V2 receptors and correct hyponatremia. Tolvaptan is hepatotoxic and should not be given to patients with liver disease. Intravenous conivaptan is very effective in correcting hyponatremia and baseline mental status in hospitalized patients. Other therapies, like lithium or demeclocycline, are also effective in SIADH, but both drugs are nephrotoxic and have other potential side effects; therefore, they should only be used when other therapies fail. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Therapeutic Uses of Diuretic Agents -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Connecting tubules are short segments of renal tubules connecting the DCT with the collecting duct. Collecting duct forms the concluding segment of the nephron and receives only about 10% of the glomerular filtrate, and the Na+ reabsorptive capacity of this segment varies significantly from 1 to 5%. [70] Aquaporin-2 is the subtype of aquaporins expressed in both connecting tubules and collecting ducts, and their density is the highest in the medullary collecting duct. The antidiuretic hormone vasopressin acts only on the aquaporin-2 subtype of aquaporin channels located in the distal nephron and does not bind to the aquaporin-1 channels located in the proximal nephron. [71]

retrieved_docs.title: Nocturia -- Treatment / Management -- Antidiuretic hormone therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Desmopressin is very similar to natural vasopressin but contains a change in 2 critical amino acids. This leaves the resulting hormone with substantial antidiuretic effects but eliminates all vasopressor activity, making it the preferred form of antidiuretic medical therapy for nocturia due to nocturnal polyuria. The use of desmopressin is usually associated with a slight increase in daytime diuresis but may lead to hyponatremia in high-risk groups. The lowest effective dose of the medication should be used, especially in older patients, but men tend to need higher dosages than women. This may be because the vasopressin V2 receptor gene is located on the X chromosome in an area relatively protected from inactivation. Overall, desmopressin therapy can reduce nocturia episodes by about 50%, as shown in 2 recent studies of 1045 patients. This effect typically takes 7 days to become clinically evident. When effective, the benefits of desmopressin therapy appear to be long-lasting. [32] Different recommended dosages exist for men and women, as females tend to be more sensitive to desmopressin.

retrieved_docs.title: Refractory Shock -- Treatment / Management -- Future Therapies
retrieved_docs.content: Selepressin: It is a synthetic selective vasopressin-V1a receptor agonist. Like vasopressin, it increases MAP and decreases the catecholamine requirements but is not associated with fluid overload from retaining water and thrombosis from the release of von Willebrand factor.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Posterior Pituitary -- Clinical Significance -- Syndrome of Inappropriate Antidiuretic Hormone
retrieved_docs.content: SIADH is a diagnosis of exclusion once all other causes of hyponatremia have been ruled out. Treatment depends on the status of the patient; if asymptomatic, then water restriction is sufficient. If this treatment fails to improve the sodium level, then add lithium or demeclocycline. Both agents antagonize antidiuretic hormone, which will induce diuresis and ultimately correct the sodium level. ADH receptor antagonists like tolvaptan and conivaptan are also approved for the treatment of SIADH. In symptomatic patients, first-line treatment depends on the severity of the symptoms. [3] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Pars Nervosa -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The two hormones released in the pars nervosa have distinct physiologic roles. Oxytocin generates smooth muscle and myoepithelial cell contraction, facilitating uterine contraction during parturition and milk letdown in lactation. AVP, also known as antidiuretic hormone (ADH), prevents consequences of volume depletion through a variety of mechanisms, each associated with a unique receptor subtype. The V1 receptor is expressed primarily on vascular smooth muscles and causes arteriolar contraction when stimulated by AVP. Expression of the V2 receptor occurs on the renal collecting ducts and tubular epithelium where AVP triggers the synthesis and expression of water transporter Aquaporin-2, increasing solute-free water reuptake and inducing a hypotonic shift in blood osmolarity. In concert, these receptors allow AVP to regulate blood tonicity and support blood pressure during volume loss. The V3 (also known as V1b) receptor is present in a variety of organs, including the anterior pituitary, where it stimulates the release of adrenocorticotropin (ACTH), beta-endorphin, and prolactin. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Desmopressin -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the indications to use desmopressin in a patient. Describe the mechanism of action of desmopressin. Review the potential adverse effects of desmopressin. Summarize the role of an integrated inter-professional team involved in the treatment of the patients receiving desmopressin. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Arginine Vasopressin Disorder (Diabetes Insipidus) -- Treatment / Management -- 2. Arginine Vasopressin Resistance (AVP-R, formerly known as Nephrogenic Diabetes Insipidus or NDI)
retrieved_docs.content: Thiazide diuretics may be used in conjunction with dietary changes. The mechanism of administering a polyuria diuretic promotes the reduction of urine volume, which triggers the endogenous release of aldosterone. By having less water delivered distally, there would be less water loss in the collecting tubule, where ADH targets its effects. [82] Other treatment options include DDAVP and NSAIDs. NSAIDs inhibit prostaglandin synthesis, which has antagonistic effects on ADH. [80]

retrieved_docs.title: Refractory Shock -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Vasopressin or antidiuretic hormone, which is stored and released from the posterior pituitary, is relatively deficient in sepsis. Vasopressin acts on V1 and V2 receptors, causing vascular smooth muscle contraction and water reabsorption in collecting tubules of the kidney. Unlike catecholamines, vasopressin does not cause arrhythmias and can work in an acidic environment. It has been shown to decrease catecholamine requirements and is associated with decreased mortality in less severe shock. Vasopressin, when used with a corticosteroid, has shown decreased mortality suggesting possible synergy. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Vasoplegic Syndrome and Noncatecholamine Therapies -- Treatment / Management -- Vasopressin
retrieved_docs.content: When high doses of catecholamines are ineffective for maintaining blood pressure or result in adverse effects, vasopressin may be utilized as an additional treatment option. Vasopressin (at doses up to 0.03 µmol/min) is used as a second-line or extra treatment for catecholamine-resistant vasodilatory shock. [19] By acting on the vasopressin receptor 1, it reinstates vascular tone. Vasopressin directly deactivates the K-ATP channel, inhibiting the increase in cGMP caused by NO, reducing NO synthesis, and minimizing the effects of membrane hyperpolarization, myosin dephosphorylation, and NO accumulation. [20] These actions make vasopressin a viable alternative when high doses of catecholamines are ineffective or unsafe.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Pituitary Hormones -- Mechanism -- Vasopressin
retrieved_docs.content: Two regulating receptors, the subfornical organ and the organum vasculosum in the hypothalamus, sense water deprivation and signal for ADH secretion. [1] A small concentration of vasopressin is sufficient to generate water conservation in the renal tubules. The renal tubules are divided into the proximal, descending, ascending, distal regions, and the collecting duct. The most ADH-dependent segment of the renal tubule is the collecting duct, which has ADH receptors on its basolateral side for ADH to bind and stimulate the Gs protein. The Gs protein stimulates adenylyl cyclase, which further converts ATP into cAMP. High levels of cAMP cause the phosphorylation of protein kinase A, subsequently opening water channels known as aquaporins to allow passage of water from the luminal side to the basolateral side.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Vasopressin -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Osmolarity and volume status are the two greatest factors that affect ADH secretion. However, a variety of other factors promote ADH secretion as well. These include angiotensin II, pain, nausea, hypoglycemia, nicotine, opiates, and certain medications. ADH secretion is also negatively affected by ethanol, alpha-adrenergic agonists, and atrial natriuretic peptide. Ethanol’s inhibitory effect helps to explain the increased diuresis experienced during intoxicated states as well as increased free water loss; without appropriate ADH secretion, the kidneys excrete more water. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Oxytocin -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Oxytocin is an oligopeptide hormone consisting of 9 amino acyl residues, making it a nonapeptide hormone. Oxytocin is 1 of the 2 hormones created in the paraventricular nucleus of the hypothalamus before being stored in or released from the posterior pituitary gland. This specific area of the posterior pituitary gland that stores oxytocin is called the pars nervosa, also known as the neural or posterior lobe. Most hormones create negative feedback loops after they are released, but oxytocin is one of the few that exhibit positive feedback; the release of oxytocin leads to actions that stimulate an even greater release of oxytocin. This feedback contrasts with antidiuretic hormone (ADH), also known as vasopressin (the other hormone stored and released from the posterior pituitary), which exhibits negative feedback after release. Less of this hormone will be released after it exhibits its effect on the body. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 640
retrieved_docs.content: Vasopressin and its analogues constitute a pharmacologic class that includes both desmopressin and terlipressin, each of which is a member of this group. These agents share structural similarity to vasopressin and act through related mechanisms, making them important therapeutic options within the vasopressin analogue category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1929
retrieved_docs.content: Conivaptan, a vasopressin receptor antagonist, exerts a physiologic effect of increased diuresis, meaning that its administration leads to enhanced urine output. This diuretic action of conivaptan can be utilized in clinical situations where promoting fluid excretion is desired, such as in the management of hyponatremia or volume overload. Understanding that increased diuresis is a direct effect of conivaptan helps clinicians anticipate its therapeutic benefits and monitor patients for appropriate fluid balance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 471
retrieved_docs.content: Vasopressin and its analogues are classified as members of the terlipresin group, indicating that terlipresin is a synthetic analogue of the endogenous hormone vasopressin and that it belongs to the same pharmacologic class as other vasopressin‑based compounds. This relationship highlights the shared mechanism of action among these agents, which act on vasopressin receptors to produce vasoconstriction and antidiuretic effects, and underscores the relevance of terlipresin as a therapeutic option within the broader category of vasopressin analogues.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 339
retrieved_docs.content: Antagonists of antidiuretic hormone constitute a pharmacological group whose molecular mechanisms involve blockade of vasopressin receptors, thereby producing diuretic effects. In the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification system these agents are placed in the “other diuretics” category, reflecting their distinct mode of action compared to traditional diuretics. Tolvaptan is a specific drug that belongs to the class of antagonists of antidiuretic hormone, illustrating that individual agents are members of this broader group. The class also has a parent classification within the larger diuretic hierarchy, linking it to the overall family of diuretic medications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1124
retrieved_docs.content: Vasopressin and its analogues are classified as hormones of the posterior pituitary lobe, where they play key roles in water retention and vascular tone regulation. Within this group, terlipresin is recognized as a specific synthetic analogue, making it a member of the broader vasopressin‑analogue class. Understanding that vasopressin and its analogues, including terlipresin, originate from posterior pituitary physiology clarifies their mechanisms of action and informs their therapeutic application in conditions such as hypotension, variceal bleeding, and certain types of shock.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1416
retrieved_docs.content: PD 134308, atosiban and tolvaptan are all classified as hormone antagonists, with a particular focus on antagonism of antidiuretic hormone (ADH). The class of antidiuretic‑hormone antagonists is itself a subset of the broader hormone‑antagonist group and is categorized within the “other diuretics” segment of the ATC (Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical) classification system. Atosiban, which is derived from the peptide vasotocin, exemplifies a substance that acts through a peptide‑hormone‑receptor antagonist mechanism and therefore belongs to the antidiuretic‑hormone antagonist subclass. Tolvaptan is a member of this antidiuretic‑hormone antagonist class, illustrating that specific drugs can be both individual agents and representatives of the larger pharmacologic group. Collectively, these relationships highlight how PD 134308, atosiban and tolvaptan are interconnected through their shared mechanism of ADH antagonism, their placement within the hormone‑antagonist hierarchy, and their relevance to diuretic therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 28
retrieved_docs.content: aquaresis without significant sodium loss. Their molecular mechanism involves selective blockade of V2 receptors in the renal collecting ducts, inhibition of antidiuretic hormone action, reduction of aquaporin‑2 insertion, and consequent aquaretic effect. Conivaptan and tolvaptan are representative agents used to treat hyponatremia and fluid overload in heart‑failure. Together, these relationships illustrate how antimetabolites, gaseous biomarkers, carbon‑based chemical entities, natriuretic peptides, and V2‑receptor antagonists are organized within a structured biomedical framework, emphasizing their interconnections and relevance for diagnosis, therapeutic intervention, and toxicological assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1323
retrieved_docs.content: Antagonisté antidiuretického hormonu constitute a pharmacological class whose actions are described under the category “farmakologické účinky – molekulární mechanismy,” reflecting their molecular mechanism of blocking the antidiuretic hormone (vasopressin) pathway. Both conivaptan and tolvaptan are specific agents that belong to this class of antidiuretic‑hormone antagonists, sharing the common mechanism of vasopressin receptor inhibition. Recognizing conivaptan and tolvaptan as members of the antagonisté antidiuretického hormonu group helps clinicians understand their therapeutic role and the underlying molecular pharmacology that guides their use in conditions such as hyponatremia and heart failure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2761
retrieved_docs.content: Antagonisté antidiuretického hormonu, the class of antidiuretic hormone antagonists, includes the drug tolvaptan. Tolvaptan is a selective vasopressin V2‑receptor antagonist that blocks the action of antidiuretic hormone, promoting free water excretion. This pharmacologic class is used in the treatment of conditions such as hyponatremia and autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease, where inhibition of vasopressin‑mediated water reabsorption provides therapeutic benefit.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 60
retrieved_docs.content: Oxytocin and its analogues are classified as posterior pituitary lobe hormones and are members of the oxytocin family. Vasopressin and its analogues are likewise posterior pituitary lobe hormones, with desmopressin and terlipresin identified as specific members of the vasopressin analogue group. Desmopressin is contraindicated in patients who have اضطراب الكلية (kidney disorder). Recognizing that both oxytocin‑related and vasopressin‑related compounds originate from the posterior pituitary informs clinical decisions about hormone replacement and the safe use of synthetic analogues, particularly avoiding desmopressin in individuals with renal impairment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 1194
retrieved_docs.content: PD 134308 is classified as a hormone antagonist. Atosiban is identified as a substance with a peptide‑hormone‑receptor antagonist mechanism of action and, through an inverse‑is‑a relationship, is also a hormone antagonist. Antidiuretic‑hormone antagonists constitute a specific subclass of hormone antagonists and are themselves a subset of substances that act as peptide‑hormone‑receptor antagonists. Within this subclass, conivaptan and tolvaptan are recognized as individual members. The antidiuretic‑hormone antagonist class is further placed under the “other diuretics” category in the ATC classification system. Together, these hierarchical relationships clarify that PD 134308 and atosiban belong to the broader hormone‑antagonist group, while conivaptan and tolvaptan represent more narrowly defined antidiuretic‑hormone antagonists, all of which are organized under the therapeutic class of other diuretics. This structured understanding aids clinicians in drug classification, mechanism‑based prescribing, and the management of conditions involving hormonal regulation of fluid balance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2811
retrieved_docs.content: Antagonisté antidiuretického hormonu are categorized as other diuretics in the ATC (Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical) classification system. By blocking the action of antidiuretic hormone, these agents increase water excretion and reduce fluid retention, making them useful in conditions where excess water balance needs to be corrected. Their placement among other diuretics reflects their role in promoting diuresis through a distinct hormonal mechanism rather than the more common electrolyte‑based pathways of classic diuretic classes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1578
retrieved_docs.content: Antagonisté antidiuretického hormonu, a class of antidiuretic hormone antagonists, comprises agents such as conivaptan and tolvaptan, both of which are members of this pharmacologic group. This class is categorized within the broader ATC group identified as other diuretics in ATC, linking antidiuretic hormone antagonists to the larger family of diuretic medications. By blocking vasopressin receptors, these agents contribute to the management of disorders associated with water retention and hyponatremia, and their classification alongside other diuretics underscores their role in fluid balance regulation within patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 632
retrieved_docs.content: which itself is defined as a subclass of potassium‑sparing diuretics (potassium‑sparing diuretics) and is likewise a member of triamteren. This hierarchical arrangement highlights that amiloride functions both as a sodium‑channel blocker and as a potassium‑sparing diuretic, reflecting its dual pharmacologic profile. Both amiloride and the vasopressin‑V2 antagonist conivaptan produce the physiologic effect of increased diuresis, underscoring their utility in conditions where enhanced urinary output is desired. Conivaptan’s mechanism of action involves inhibition of the cytochrome P450 3A4 enzyme (Cytochrome P450 3A4 Inhibitors [MoA]), linking it to a well‑known metabolic pathway that can affect the disposition of many drugs. Accordingly, conivaptan is contraindicated in patients with urinary retention (انقطاع البول), a restriction it shares with the β‑blocker bisoprolol, which is also listed as contraindicated in this condition. Finerenone, a mineralocorticoid‑receptor antagonist, is

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 1081
retrieved_docs.content: are identified as inverse subclasses of the therapeutic‑role classification, reflecting their specialized functions. Finally, the pharmacological group “antagonisté antidiuretického hormonu” (antidiuretic‑hormone antagonists) occupies a parent position within the molecular‑mechanism classification, is placed in the ATC “other diuretics” group, and comprises agents such as conivaptan and tolvaptan. This group’s effects are described by the molecular mechanisms termed “farmakologické účinky – molekulární mechanismy.”

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 775
retrieved_docs.content: Antacids are contraindicated for use with the famotidine class of H₂‑receptor antagonists, indicating that concurrent administration should be avoided. Antacids, alkylating agents, and antagonisté antidiuretického hormonu (antidiuretic hormone antagonists) each belong to broader pharmacological categories defined by their molecular mechanisms, reflecting a hierarchical “has parent” relationship within drug action pathways. Antagonisté antidiuretického hormonu include agents such as tolvaptan, a vasopressin V₂‑receptor antagonist, illustrating that tolvaptan is a member of this drug class. Recognizing these interconnections helps clinicians prevent drug‑drug interactions, understand the mechanistic basis of therapy, and select appropriate medications for patient care.
